dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 respond to 123 Main for a 62-year-old male with chest pain.
Hi, I'm Alex, EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Chest pain started about 30 minutes ago, and I'm not sure what else is going on.
Are you awake and aware of where you are right now, sir?
Yes, your eyes are open and you are responding clearly.
You're able to communicate coherently and are alert to your surroundings.
I'll ask you to squeeze my fingers and move your arms. Can you do that for me?
You mentioned chest pain-do you have any other medical conditions we should know about?
I have a history of high blood pressure. I've been drinking, but I can't remember exactly how much.
Your skin is pink and dry with no breaks or rashes found.
Head and neck appear normal with no signs of injury or deformity. No tracheal deviation.
No discharge from nasal passages and mouth is clear.
Your airway is open and you appear able to manage it successfully.
Breathing is equal on both sides with no chest asymmetry. No signs of cyanosis.
We will now check for peripheral pulses and skin condition.
Blood pressure is 182 over 120.
Heart rate is 80 beats per minute and regular.
Respiratory rate is 20 beats per minute with even chest rise and fall.
Oxygen saturation is 97% as measured.
Blood glucose at 135 mg/dL.
Initial EKG shows a normal sinus rhythm.
How would you describe the chest pain? Is it a sharp, dull, or burning sensation?
A 12-lead EKG was performed, but initial readings were inconclusive due to movement and artifacts.
Your chest is expanding symmetrically with normal, clear breath sounds on both sides.
You indicated you've been drinking. How long have you been outside today?
We are continuing 4-lead ECG monitoring for arrhythmia detection.
Retaking 4-lead EKG to ensure accurate rhythm monitoring.
Your blood pressure is now 153 over 82.
Oxygen has improved slightly to 98%.
Your pulse has decreased to 70 beats per minute, still within normal range.
The second 12-lead showed abnormal signs possibly indicating an infarct, but the third confirmed a normal sinus rhythm.
We will now take you to Retreat Hospital for further evaluation at the emergency department.
Your current blood pressure is 155 over 91.
Glucose is again reading zero, but you remain alert.
Blood pressure is 140 over 110 now.
Oxygen saturation at 95% remains consistent.
Pulse is 68 beats per minute.
You're now being placed on the stretcher using a five-point harness for safe transport.
ED nurse, we have a 62-year-old with a past history of hypertension, intoxicated, and possible EKG changes from MI.
Initial fast heart rate seems to have resolved.
No signs of bruising, rashes, or other abnormalities observed during the skin scan.
Airway is clear and maintainable.
No signs of dyspnea or cyanosis. Work of breathing is normal.
No medications on board.
No known allergies for this patient.
Can you tell me what happened or led up to your calling 911?
Upright and conscious position seems ideal to maintain airway.
Have you ever felt chest discomfort prior to today?
You appear oriented to person, time, and place, but mental status is altered.
No signs of ear or nasal obstruction found.
Lung fields are clear bilaterally with symmetrical chest movement.
Heart sounds were clear upon auscultation with no noted abnormalities.
No observed deformities or angulation; extremities appear intact and functional.
What time exactly did you first experience the chest pain?
Does any specific movement or activity make the pain worse?
You said it is a sharp feeling. Does it feel like a burning or squeezing at times?
Have you noticed the pain radiate into your neck or arms at all?
Using a 0-to-10 scale, how would you rate the intensity of your pain now?
Has the duration or characteristics changed from when you first noticed it?
The third 12-lead reading ruled out an inferior infarct, confirming a normal sinus rhythm.
Full visual inspection of the skin showed no breaks or rashes.
Seat harness is secure. You are safely ready for transport now.
Confirmatory blood pressure reading at 155 over 91.
As of now, your oxygen saturation is at 100%.
We should arrive at the hospital in about ten minutes. You're in good care.
Patient was conscious and alert with no acute signs of distress.
Transporting under EMS Protocol 5-1 for hypothermic conditions.
Can you describe any back or neck discomfort? Visible signs of spine injury are absent.
The safety straps are in place-should be comfortable for you.
Readjusting the stretcher to load into the ambulance.
Pulse is now 67 beats per minute, unchanged from last reading.
Confirming blood pressure remains at 140 over 110.
Oxygen saturation is 95% with no change noted.
Glucose is still zero, but patient is alert and oriented.
Latest EKG confirms stable normal sinus rhythm with no arrhythmia.
No signs of tachycardia in most recent readings.
While initial readings concerned us, your recent vitals show a stable trend without cardiac changes.
Hypothermic condition confirmed. Using a five-point system for secure transport.
You were laughing during procedures. This is often a sign of alcohol intoxication.
It's a longer ride, but you'll be in the ED room soon for further assessment.
The ED has been informed of potential vital sign concerns and movement incidents.
All monitoring systems are clear. Ready to depart.
Patient is lying stable with the harness in place during the ride.
Our reassessment confirms sharp pain is localized, no spread found.
At this time, severity remains unchanged with no report of increased intensity.
BP readings have settled into a stable range with no symptom progression.
Skin is dry, condition is alert, and no acute troubles confirmed during secondary checks.
Approximately five more minutes until we reach Retreat; you can get settled.
Mental status: Alert but showing a slightly delayed response time.
Final check of all restraints in place and ready for transport.
Anything else you'd like to know before we head out?
All readings and procedures are in compliance and ready to move out.
Ambulance starting for en route to Retreat, where your evaluation will continue.
Heart rate is at 68, and blood pressure stays 140/110. Oxygen is at 95%.
Glucose is still zero, and patient remains alert.
Most recent EKG scan was stable with no QT concerns.
We'll arrive at the hospital shortly for full ED review.
Arrived at Retreat for patient pickup and ongoing care.
ED team, primary EKG showed possible MI, but stable subsequent scans.
Stretcher secured. ED team in place for transfer.
Initiating sheet lift protocol for ED bed transfer.
ED nurse, here's the full transfer justification, findings, and status updates.
Patient officially handed over for full ED care.
Report documentation completed and handover finalized.
Signature accepted. Thank you for the support and complete transfer.
Situating you upright to reduce airway influence from hypothermia.
Skin on extremities appears closer in temperature-cool to touch, consistent with field assessment.
Ambient temperature was low, likely contributing to hypothermia. Keeping patient covered during transport.
Oxygen saturation dropped again to 95% mid-ride-confirming supplemental oxygen needs.
Patient shows altered mental status and delayed orientation-likely from intoxication and hypothermia.
Final EKG displayed possible ST depression, but patient continued showing normal rhythm. Clinically stable.
Pallor in the cheeks and INNEREar rims noted-possible signs of vasoconstriction, but no other symptoms.
ED staff, patient was caught with pallor, hypothermia, and alcohol intoxication-responding to EMS care per standard.
